text: The surgeon prepares for a laparoscopic myomectomy by using a supraumbilical port for panoramic visualization. Irrigation and suction are performed, followed by the hanging drop test to confirm pneumoperitoneum.

So laparoscopic myomectomy in this patient was a large two myomas and entire cavity was almost obliterated due to the deep grade two intramural myoma. So in those situation you have to do the surgery very nicely and we should avoid opening the cavity that is one of the one of the care which we should must take because if cavity is open then of course you can suture it there is no problem but in spite of suturing there is a chance that it may get obliterated or there may be some adhesions so let us see what happened here we are using the supram lycal port and now this is the ten millimeter port which will be introduced

The surgeon positions three laparoscopic ports: one supraumbilical and two ipsilateral, arranged in a baseball diamond configuration. The ports are placed 7.5 centimeters lateral and 7.5 centimeters below the umbilicus, completing the setup for the myomectomy procedure.

The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position, revealing a large intramural myoma. The surgeon injects diluted vasopressin into the muscle layer, creating a marble white appearance indicative of transient ischemia.

The surgeon begins cutting the muscle layer using harmonic or monopolar instruments, creating a large incision to facilitate separation. The myoma starts to become visible as it pops out from the muscle layer.

The surgeon completes the incision through the muscle layer, revealing the myoma protruding from the tissue. Another myoma is visible on top, attached to the first. The bladder appears slightly distended, and the surgeon prepares to use a myoma screw for manipulation.

The surgeon attaches the myoma screw near the base of the myoma to provide proper traction. Using a pushing and pulling technique, the myoma is enucleated symmetrically from all sides. The myoma screw is periodically rearranged to maintain optimal traction. The surgical field shows the cavity, with care taken to preserve the endometrial and basal layers during separation.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy using three ports, optimizing traction and counter-traction by holding the myoma screw. The bulge of the cavity is visible, and care is taken to maintain its integrity without opening it.
The surgeon rearranges the myoma screw to optimize traction and counter-traction, reaching the base of the myoma. Both myomas are visibly attached to each other.

The surgeon carefully separates the myoma, revealing the bulge of the cavity beneath. The endometrial layer remains intact, allowing for suturing. A number one vicryl suture with a co-op needle is introduced through the serosa, focusing on approximating the muscle layer with continuous suturing while avoiding pricking the cavity.

The surgeon performs a deep bite suture from muscle to muscle, using the left hand to maintain traction on the suture, which acts as a retractor. The needle exits through the serosa, allowing the surgeon to suture the serosa layer. The needle is returned through the same path, maintaining a one-centimeter distance from the margin, which tightens previous sutures and inverts the margins slightly.

The surgeon pulls the suture tail to tighten the inner layer further and ties a surgeon's knot to terminate the suturing. The two-layer continuous suturing is completed, and the next step is morselation of the large myoma, measuring twelve centimeters. The morselation process is slow and requires careful attention.

The surgeon carefully performs morcellation, ensuring the tissue is brought to the morcellator rather than pushing the morcellator onto the tissue. This technique prevents overshooting and minimizes the risk of injury to surrounding structures such as the bowel, sigmoid colon, mesentery, or omentum.

The laparoscopic view shows that approximately twenty-five percent of the fibroid tissue has been morcellated, with seventy-five percent remaining. The surgeon carefully continues the morcellation process, ensuring correct ergonomics and port positioning.
